note_data.title: Ideal gases
keyPoints: Force fields are areas where objects experience non-contact forces, formed during interaction of masses, static charges or moving charges
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
There are two types of gravitational fields: uniform fields (same force everywhere) and radial fields (force depends on position)
Gravitational field strength (g) is the force per unit mass, calculated using g = F/m
Newton's law of gravitation states gravitational force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them
Gravitational force follows F = Gm₁m₂/r² where G is gravitational constant, m₁/m₂ are masses and r is distance between centres
For radial fields, gravitational field strength follows g = GM/r² showing an inverse square relationship
Mass of a uniform sphere acts as a point mass at its centre when calculating gravitational force outside the sphere

definitions.definition: An area in which an object experiences a non-contact force
An area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object
Exerts the same gravitational force on a mass everywhere in the field
The force exerted depends on the position of the object in the field

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all algebraic steps clearly, stating any assumptions made
Show working, include units, give answer to appropriate significant figures

modelAnswer.question: Calculate the root mean square speed of nitrogen molecules at 27°C. (Molar mass of nitrogen = 28.0 g mol⁻¹, R = 8.31 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹)
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Convert temperature to Kelvin: T = 300 K
Mark 2: Substitute values into formula c = √(3RT/M)
Mark 3: Calculate final answer with correct units: 517 m s⁻¹

commonMistakes: Forgetting that gravitational field strength follows an inverse square relationship - if distance increases by factor of 2, field strength decreases by factor of 4
Not recognizing that gravity always acts as attractive force between masses
Confusing when to treat spherical masses as point masses - only applies when calculating force on objects outside the sphere
examinerTips: Remember that closer field lines indicate stronger gravitational field strength
Earth's gravitational field is radial but appears uniform very close to the surface
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Molecular motion diagram showing gas molecules as point masses moving randomly in all directions within a cubic container, with arrows indicating elastic collisions with walls
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the kinetic theory assumptions and calculate pressure using the ideal gas equation
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing understanding of which gases behave most like ideal gases at room temperature
M/J 2022 P2 Q3(b) (4 marks) — students had to explain why real gases deviate from ideal behavior at high pressure and low temperature
O/N 2021 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — calculation question involving changes in pressure, volume and temperature using combined gas laws
M/J 2021 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — tested understanding of the relationship between kinetic energy and absolute temperature

workedExample.question: A sample of gas occupies 200 cm³ at 27°C and 1.5 × 10⁵ Pa. Calculate the volume occupied by the same gas at 127°C and 3.0 × 10⁵ Pa. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin: T₁ = 27 + 273 = 300 K, T₂ = 127 + 273 = 400 K
Step 2: Identify known values: V₁ = 200 cm³, P₁ = 1.5 × 10⁵ Pa, P₂ = 3.0 × 10⁵ Pa, T₁ = 300 K, T₂ = 400 K
Step 3: Apply combined gas law: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂
workedExample.answer: The gas occupies 133 cm³ at the new conditions.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for temperature conversion, 1 mark for correct equation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer with units

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to convert Celsius to Kelvin' — always convert temperature to Kelvin (add 273) when using gas law equations

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students confused the assumptions of kinetic theory with properties of real gases' — clearly distinguish between ideal gas assumptions (no intermolecular forces, negligible molecular volume) and real gas behavior
Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often omitted units in gas law calculations' — always include appropriate units (Pa for pressure, m³ or cm³ for volume, K for temperature) in final answers
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a molecular model showing gas particles as small circles or dots randomly distributed in a container with arrows indicating random motion in all directions. The diagram should show particles well-spaced (to illustrate negligible molecular volume compared to container volume) and moving in straight lines between collisions with walls. Include labels for 'random motion', 'elastic collisions with walls', and 'negligible intermolecular forces'.
note_data.quickRecap: Gravitational fields are regions where masses experience non-contact forces. Field strength follows g = F/m and varies according to g = GM/r² in radial fields. Newton's law of gravitation shows force is proportional to mass product and inversely proportional to distance squared. Gravity is always attractive and spherical masses act as point masses at their centres for external calculations.
